content.problemStatement:
You have a rectangular floor RRR with width WWW and length LLL. You want to tile this floor with some rectangular tiles - R1,R2,…,RkR_1, R_2, \ldots, R_kR1​,R2​,…,Rk​, where kkk is any integer ≥1\ge 1≥1.

But you want to tile it such that for iii, 1≤i≤k1 \leq i \leq k1≤i≤k, the width  and height of RiR_iRi​ is a positive integer, and RiR_iRi​ has a perimeter which isn't divisible by 444. 

Note that different tiles can have different heights and widths.

You need to find out whether this is possible to do so, or not.

**Note:** RRR is said to be tiled by R1,R2,…,RkR_1, R_2, \ldots, R_kR1​,R2​,…,Rk​, if


Every bit of RRR is covered by exactly one RiR_iRi​ (which also means that no two RiR_iRi​ and RjR_jRj​ should overlap), and
Every RiR_iRi​ is completely within RRR. That is, none of them stick out.
This is an example of a valid tiling:


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers WWW, LLL — denoting the width and length of the rectangular floor.


Output Format
For each test case, output `YES` if it's possible to tile the floor using rectangular tiles of integral sides and perimeter not divisible by 444. Otherwise, output `NO`.


You may print each character of `YES` and `NO` in uppercase or lowercase (for example, `yes`, `yEs` and `Yes` will all be considered identical).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers W and L — the width and length of the floor.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if the floor can be tiled with rectangles of integer sides whose perimeter is not divisible by 4, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: 4*6=24 is even → YES.\nTest case 2: 2*5=10 is even → YES.\nTest case 3: 3*3=9 is odd → NO.
Product is even because both numbers are even.

solution.approach: A rectangle of sides a and b has perimeter 2*(a+b). The perimeter is divisible by 4 iff a+b is even. Therefore each allowed tile must satisfy a+b odd, i.e., its two sides have opposite parity. Such a tile always has even area (even * odd). Consequently the sum of areas of all tiles (the whole floor) must be even. The floor area is W*L, which is even exactly when at least one of W or L is even. If the area is even we can simply tile the floor with 1×2 dominoes (1+2 is odd, perimeter 6 not divisible by 4). If the area is odd, tiling is impossible because the total area would be a sum of even numbers, which can never be odd. Hence answer is YES iff W or L is even.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
